old_claim.note: Mechanical Theater Machinery provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No theater-machinery prerequisite remains because the documented automaton is driven by integrated spring clockwork rather than stage equipment.

source_shape.source_locator: Object description and curator's comments document the circa-1585 automaton's springs, clock, gears, programme barrel, moving figures, bellows, music, and cannon sequence.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The museum identifies a self-contained clockwork mechanism as the source of the Mechanical Galleon's sequenced movement and effects.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The complete mechanism account supplies no stage apparatus or theater lineage, while the former citation discussed escapements and could not support that edge.

ontology_before: Court automata were made downstream of the separately scoped Italian perspective-stage machinery tradition.
ontology_after: Court automata remain on the mechanical-clock branch without importing an unrelated theatrical production system.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: clockwork_automata no longer directly depends on mechanical_theater_machinery.
would_reject_if: Workshop evidence established that Schlottheim's scoped automata derived their mechanisms from Renaissance stage machinery rather than clockmaking practice.
short_reason: The object record documents integrated clockwork and no theater-machinery dependency.
